title: 26  Inside Shmup: A Frame of Reference
section: 26.5 A boss is not a big enemy
text:
26.5 A boss is not a big enemy
It is tempting to model a boss as an enemy with a lot of hit points, and for about ten minutes that works. Then you want it to enter from off-screen without being culled; to be immune while it enters; to change its fire pattern when it is hurt; to hold the wave timeline open until it dies; and to be the ending. None of that is “more HP”. All of it is a small state machine with a phase list, which is what core/boss.lua is: enter → fight → dying, with Boss.vulnerable() — a single predicate, Boss.active and Boss.state == "fight" — so that no caller has to remember the rule.


boss.lua

local function phase()
    local sp = Boss.spec
    local frac = Boss.hp / Boss.maxHp
    for _, p in ipairs(sp.phases) do
        if frac > (p.above or 0) then return p end
    end
    return sp.phases[#sp.phases]
end

function Boss.damage(dmg)
    if not Boss.vulnerable() then return false end
    Boss.hp = Boss.hp - dmg
    Boss.hit = 0.08
    if Boss.hp <= 0 then
        Boss.hp = 0
        Boss.state = "dying"
        Boss.t = 0
        Fx.shake(7)
        Snd.bossDie()
        return true
    end
    Snd.hit()
    return false
end

Phases are picked by remaining HP fraction, not by a timer and not by a counter: the first phase in the spec whose above the fraction still exceeds. Nova’s dreadnought has two — a stately sweep with a five-shot spread you can out-position, and, below half health, the desperate one that stops aiming and starts filling the screen with rings. The phase list is data in the content table, so a boss’s whole personality is written where the level is written.
The death cascade is deliberate too: dying scatters an explosion across the hull every 0.12 s for 1.6 seconds before the level ends. The pause is the point. It is the one moment in the game when nothing is trying to kill you.
And then the rule that keeps all of it honest:


shmup.lua

-- THE win condition, per stage. If the stage has a FINAL boss, that boss is
-- the ending -- full stop (mid-bosses gate the road, not the stage). Only a
-- stage with no final boss falls back to "the spawn script ran out".
local function stageWon()
    if Waves.hasBoss then return finalBossDown end
    return Waves.finished()
end

The final boss owns the win condition. If the level has one, it is cleared when that boss dies — never when the spawn script happens to run dry. Only a level with no boss falls back to “the script is exhausted and the screen is swept” (Waves.finished). Without that rule you get the old engine’s ending, in which the level declares victory the instant the last scripted grunt drifts off the bottom edge, several seconds after the player stopped doing anything. A level that ends by accident is read by the player as a bug, and they are not wrong.
The engine has since grown a campaign layer — several stages back to back — so the predicate is named stageWon and the latch it waits on is finalBossDown. That latch is the home of one more distinction: a wave entry marked mid = true arms a mid-boss, one that fights and dies partway through the level to gate the road without being the ending. Waves.hasBoss counts only the final, non-mid boss, so a mid-boss can die on camera without tripping the win.






Figure 26.5: The Dreadnought at frame 400, mid-ring, in its second phase. The health bar at the foot of the screen is the fight’s clock: without it the player cannot tell progress from stalemate, and a boss that feels like a stalemate is a boss they quit to the launcher on.




26.5.1 The bug you can only see by looking
The boss used to have a hit flash. The enemies have one and it works beautifully — Sprites.draw(name, x, y, flash) fills the silhouette solid white for two frames, and against the black field it reads instantly as “that one is taking damage”. So the boss got the same treatment.
The boss is under sustained fire. The autopilot alone hits it about eight times a second. The flash never let go.


boss.lua

-- NO hit flash. The enemies get one -- fill the silhouette solid, it blooms
-- for two frames and reads as damage -- but a boss is under sustained fire:
-- it takes a hit roughly eight times a second, so the "flash" never lets
-- go, and the thing you carefully drew renders as a featureless white
-- rectangle for the entire fight. (This was invisible in every counter. It
-- took one look at a screenshot.) Damage is legible from the health bar,
-- the impact sound, and the bullets winking out against the hull.
Sprites.draw(Boss.spec.sprite, Frame.toScreenX(Boss.x), Boss.y)

For the entire fight — the climax of the game, the thing with the hand-drawn gun ports and the phase list and the health bar — the boss rendered as a featureless white rectangle. Every counter was healthy. bossHp fell, wins reached 1, the smoke run was green, the frame time was fine. Nothing in the instrumentation could possibly have known, because nothing in the instrumentation looks at the screen.
One glance at a screenshot could. This is Chapter 18’s standing warning in its purest form: a heartbeat tells you the game is running, and it will tell you that with total confidence while the game is unplayable. The counter is a smoke detector, not an inspector. Look at the picture. That is why every smoke run in this book writes a PNG.
crumbs: 26  Inside Shmup: A Frame of Reference

title: 26  Inside Shmup: A Frame of Reference
text:
26.6 Autopilots written against the frame
Every game in the fleet ships a bot that plays it, because that bot is the smoke test (Chapter 18). Shmup’s three raise the bar in one specific way, and tools/smoke.sh enforces it:
# shmup/tools/smoke.sh:50
        grep -q '"wins":1' "$DATA/smoke.json" 2>/dev/null && break
A run is green only when the bot reached the win screen. Not survived, not scored, not “no errors” — won, boss included. The reason is the same one Chapter 25 learned from a bot that farmed a boss’s summons for 57 kills and never touched the boss: a bot that merely survives has quietly stopped testing the second half of the game, and it looks exactly like a bot that is working. wins is a latch in shmup.lua, incremented once and never reset, because the bot presses A on the victory screen and starts a fresh run — a counter that reported only the current state would answer “no” a second later, and a green smoke run that forgot it won is worse than no smoke run at all.
What makes the three bots worth reading is that each one is shaped by its frame. They are not three difficulty settings on one bot; they are three different games to play.

26.6.1 Seed it, or you are not testing anything
Before any of that, one line had to change, and it is the most valuable line in the chapter.
Shmup.run seeds the RNG from the clock, which is right for a shipped game and catastrophic for a test. Seeded from the clock, every smoke run plays a different game: the drops differ, so the weapon level differs, so the boss fight is short or long, so the bot lives or dies. Ravine’s bot sat at seven wins in eight for an afternoon — which is the worst result available, because it is too good to look broken and too flaky to trust. A green run proved nothing, a red one was indistinguishable from bad luck, and so every “fix” was being evaluated against a coin.


shmup.lua

-- A shipped game seeds from the clock. A SMOKE build must not: seeded from
-- the clock, every run is a different game, so a green run proves nothing
-- and a red one is indistinguishable from bad luck. (This engine's bot
-- passed seven runs in eight, which is the worst possible result: too good
-- to look broken, too flaky to trust.) The Makefile writes SMOKE_SEED and
-- tools/smoke.sh sweeps a few of them, so a failure is a fact you can
-- reproduce by name rather than a mood.
if SMOKE_BUILD then
    math.randomseed(SMOKE_SEED or 1)
else
    math.randomseed(playdate.getSecondsSinceEpoch())
end

The build now writes SMOKE_SEED, tools/smoke.sh sweeps several of them, and the bot must win all of them. The effect was immediate and slightly humiliating: with the seeds pinned, Nova’s bot — which had been passing happily all week — turned out to lose two seeds out of four. It had never been good. It had been lucky, and the clock had been covering for it.
This is the general form of a very common self-deception. If a test’s inputs are not pinned, its passes are not evidence. Pin them, and a failure stops being a mood and becomes a fact with a name you can type again.


26.6.2 Three bots, and one mistake made three times
The vertical bot (Nova) holds the bottom edge and picks a column. The side bot (Ravine) flies the cavern and picks an altitude. Both began life as reflexes — find the incoming fire, lean the other way — and both failed, in three escalating ways worth knowing, because everybody writes this function eventually:

Lean away from where the bullet is. Wrong: it is moving. A bullet currently beside you is a bullet about to be on you. Solve for when it crosses your row, and use its position then.
Sum the leans from several bullets. Wrong: caught between two of them, the votes cancel to zero and the bot holds a perfectly straight line through the crossfire. (A tie-break patches this. It does not cure it.)
Recompute the lean every frame. Wrong, and this one is nasty: the bot slides just far enough that the threat stops registering, the lean drops to zero, it steers calmly back toward the middle of the gap — and back into the bullet, which has not gone anywhere. It oscillates its way into every shot fired at it.

The death-cause counters said so in one word, four times over: bullet, bullet, bullet, bullet. Not the wall. Not a hull. I had spent an hour hardening the terrain clamp of a bot that was never once hitting the terrain — which is exactly why the engine now counts what killed you, by cause, in the one place every hit already passes through.
The cure is to stop reacting and start choosing. The reachable space is one-dimensional and small — a 150-pixel gap, or the stretch of screen you can cross in half a second — so score every position in it: how close the incoming fire will pass to it at the moment that fire arrives, plus the cost of flying there, plus a pull toward whatever you would like to shoot. Take the best. It is thirty lines, it costs a couple of hundred comparisons a frame, and it cannot oscillate, because the cost of moving is part of what it is minimising.
-- shmup/games/ravine/main.lua:209
            local score = -danger * 4 - math.abs(y - Player.y) * 0.3
            if aim then score = score - math.abs(y - aim) * 0.4 end
            if score > bestScore then bestScore, best = score, y end
Two footnotes, both paid for. The first: Nova’s version must consider only columns it can actually reach, because searching the whole 400-pixel screen made it strictly worse — it kept electing to be somewhere lovely and dying on the way there. Ravine can search its entire gap, because the gap is 150 pixels tall and the trip is free. The second: that aim weight is a knife edge. Too high and the bot parks directly under the boss, which is precisely where a boss’s fire is densest. Too low and it stops committing to a firing line at all — Nova scored 1550 and died with the boss on 49 HP, having spent the whole level gracefully dodging things it never shot. A bot that will not commit does not run out of luck. It runs out of level.
And the tuning cut both ways, which is the honest part. What made Nova’s boss unbeatable was not only the bot. Its desperate phase fired a full 360-degree ring — and a ring, from a boss at the top of the screen, puts its densest fire directly beneath itself, which is the one place you have to stand in order to shoot back. That is not difficulty; that is a game asking the player to choose between attacking and living. A wide downward spread threatens the same width of screen but leaves pockets you can be in. The bot went from dying with the boss on 49 HP to winning with all three lives intact — and so, I suspect, would you.
The side bot steers by Terrain.groundY / Terrain.ceilY — which is to say, by the same sampled profile the collider uses:
-- shmup/games/ravine/main.lua:68
    local function gap()
        local ahead = math.min(SCREEN_W - 1, Player.x + 46)
        local mid = (Terrain.groundY(ahead) + Terrain.ceilY(ahead)) / 2

        local lo, hi = -1e9, 1e9        -- worst ceiling, worst ground
        for x = Player.x, ahead, 12 do
            lo = math.max(lo, Terrain.ceilY(x))
            hi = math.min(hi, Terrain.groundY(x))
        end
        -- (zone 3 also clamps lo/hi against the pistons' worst stroke)
        return mid, lo, hi
    end
That is not a convenience. It is the terrain rule stated as an executable proof. A bot that read the true sine instead — the “correct” curve, the one the old collider used — would fly what it believed was the exact centre of the gap and would still, occasionally, clip a wall it was nowhere near. And it would do it rarely enough to look like flakiness rather than like a bug, which is how the original survived. It aims a little way ahead (+46 pixels) because at 66 px/s the wall you have to clear is one you can already see, and it solves a ballistic drop point for its bombs — fall time from the ship’s altitude to the target’s, then the x at which a bomb launched now meets a tank that is itself sliding left, closing at the sum of the bomb’s drift and the world’s speed.
The free bot (Skimmer) is the only one of the three that can fail by being slow. That is the free frame’s whole character: the level is a place with a far end, so there is somewhere to get to. The scroller bots cannot loiter — the world drags them forward whether they like it or not — but this one has to decide to go, and a bot that merely survived would hover happily at the near end of the hull forever and report a perfectly green run. So the bot’s spine is one variable — goal — and one instruction: fly toward it.
-- shmup/games/skimmer/main.lua:176
        -- WHERE TO BE. The far end of the hull — unless a core is live, in
        -- which case hold station 145 short of it (the stand-off doubles as
        -- "keep the whole boss on screen": the camera centres the player, so
        -- distance and visibility are the same number in the free frame) —
        -- or a closed bulkhead door is ahead, in which case hold short and
        -- settle the argument with the warden first.
        local goal
        if Boss.active then
            goal = Boss.x - 145
        else
            goal = Frame.levelW - 60
            local g = Level.gate
            if g and not g.open and Player.x < g.x then
                goal = math.min(goal, g.x - 70)
            end
        end
That comment is the free frame in one paragraph: because the camera is bound to the player, distance and visibility stop being independent variables. The bot’s other lesson is a constraint-versus-preference one that any navigation code can use. A girder does not dictate an altitude; it forbids a band. The first version treated it as a command (“fly to the far side”) and switched dodging off while a girder was ahead — so in the boss arena, where rings come at you from every angle, the bot obediently held a straight line and died. Compute the open corridor first, then choose freely inside it.






NoteThe bot reads only what a player can see



Each of the three reads the enemy pool, the bullet pool, the boss and the terrain — the same state the renderer draws. None of them reads the wave script, the RNG, or anything about the future. That discipline is what keeps an autopilot honest as a test: a bot that peeks at the spawn table proves that the spawn table exists, and nothing else. And it is what lets the same bot survive a content change: retune Nova’s waves and its bot re-derives its plan from what is on the screen.


The example’s own bot is a fourth one, in main.lua, and it makes the point more bluntly than any of them: it is a single function that branches on Frame.mode and plays accordingly. Not on the enemy list, not on the level, not on the game — on the frame. That is the clearest possible statement of what a frame actually changes: not the rules, not the bullets, not the enemies. Just where you are allowed to be, and what “forward” means.
crumbs: 26  Inside Shmup: A Frame of Reference

title: 26  Inside Shmup: A Frame of Reference
text:
26.7 The furniture
The rest of core/ is the fleet’s standard cabinet, and it is short. Two of its choices are worth a paragraph each.
Sprites are solid white, code-drawn at load, and there is not one image file in the package. This is the OpenTyrian 1-bit rule (Chapter 4, Chapter 6), and in a shmup it is not an aesthetic preference but a legibility requirement: at bullet size, the eye cannot resolve a dither pattern — it resolves the average, and the average of a 50 % dither is the mid-grey smudge that a bullet must never be. Dither is for the Dither engine’s big soft shapes (Chapter 24). A shmup is small fast shapes, and small fast shapes must be solid. The one place the rule bites back is the terrain, which is a solid white silhouette — so Kit.panel puts a black plate under the HUD, because white text on a white cavern wall is not text.
The engine had no audio at all before the rewrite; it now has both halves of the book’s sound chapters. snd.lua is a round-robin pool of three synths per waveform (Chapter 12), and the pool is not premature optimization: a shmup fires a lot of bullets, and a single shared synth turns rapid fire into one long stutter as each shot steals the voice from the one before it. music.lua is the clock-driven 16-step sequencer from Chapter 13, mixed deliberately quiet, because in this genre the sound effects are information and the music is only weather. Kit.saveBest writes the high score only when it is actually beaten (Chapter 17), so a losing run costs zero datastore writes. And fx.lua is screen shake plus hitstop (Chapter 15), with the HUD drawn after Fx.pop() — a shaking world reads as an explosion, a shaking HUD reads as a rendering bug.
Finally, the loop, which is the last thing you need to believe the thesis:


main.lua

local phase = 0

local function phaseAt(frame)
    if frame >= DIAG_AT then return 4 end
    if frame >= FREE_AT then return 3 end
    if frame >= SIDE_AT then return 2 end
    return 1
end

local frame = 0
function playdate.update()
    frame = frame + 1
    Harness.frame(frame, function()
        local p = phaseAt(frame)
        if p ~= phase then
            phase = p
            -- The whole game is swapped out here and the engine does not
            -- notice, because a level IS its content table.
            if p <= 3 then Shmup.new(Demo.levels[p]) end
        end

        if phase == 4 then
            Demo.drawProfile()
            return
        end

        Shmup.update(DT)
        Shmup.draw()
    end)
end

The demo swaps the entire game out from under the engine three times in 1,120 frames — a different frame, a different enemy set, a different level each time — with one call to Shmup.new. The engine does not notice, because a level is its content table.
crumbs: 26  Inside Shmup: A Frame of Reference

title: 26  Inside Shmup: A Frame of Reference
text:
26.8 What you know now

The five engines before this one each chose a representation. Shmup chose a frame of reference, because “what is a shoot-’em-up made of?” is the wrong question and “what is nailed down and what moves?” is the right one.
frame.lua owns exactly four questions — toScreenX (where frame coordinates land), advance (how the world moves), bounds (where the player may be), fireDir (which way is forward) — and nothing else in the engine is allowed to answer them. Three modes cover the genre; choosing one is a single string in a content table.
Frame.spent and Frame.cull are different questions. A bullet is spent against the visible box; an enemy is culled against the world — and in the free frame nothing is ever culled, because the level is a place that exists whether or not you are looking at it.
One shape, one source of truth. The cavern is sampled once; Terrain.draw fills its polygon from the samples and Terrain.hits interpolates within them. Draw a shape one way and collide it another and the two will disagree — by under a pixel, which is precisely why the bug is poison: invisible in a screenshot, absent from every counter, and felt as an unfair death nobody can reproduce.
A pool is only as good as its coldest field. Pool:spawn clears the slot’s data sub-table in place; the way to audit a pool is to read every spawn call site looking for a {}. And draw with :eachLive, because collision marks the dead mid-frame and :each paints a corpse on top of its own explosion for exactly one frame.
A boss is a phase state machine, and it owns the win condition. Immune while entering, never culled, phases chosen by remaining HP fraction, a death cascade — and a level with a boss is cleared when its final boss dies (a mid-boss gates the road but is not the ending), never when the spawn script happens to run dry. A level that ends by accident is read as a bug.
The autopilot must be able to win. All three bots beat their game, boss included, and smoke.sh passes only on "wins":1 — because a bot that merely survives has stopped testing the second half of the game. Each bot is shaped by its frame: the vertical one leads its dodges (a bot that compared current x died to the boss every run), the side one flies the gap by reading the same profile the collider reads, and the free one is the only one that can fail by being slow.
Counters cannot see the screen. The boss’s hit flash never let go under sustained fire and the climax of the game rendered as a white rectangle, with every counter green. Look at the picture.

The engine and all three games are MIT-licensed at github.com/plaidate/shmup — read DESIGN.md, then frame.lua, which is the whole thesis in one short file, then any one of the three content.lua files, which are the same game with one word changed.
And that closes Part VII, and the book. Six packages, six theses: Phosphor draws lines and nothing else; Tiles makes the grid the single source of truth; Voxel earns its volume by demanding that a game dig, climb, lob or fall; Dither promotes shade from an art trick to a mechanic; Lore keeps a story over a world; and Shmup fixes a frame of reference and lets everything else be the same code. Five of them chose what the world is made of. The sixth chose what is nailed down. The common thread is not the cabinet they share, or the 30 fps, or the procedural art, or the bots that beat their own games before anyone was allowed to say they worked — those are consequences. The thread is that an engine is a decision about what your games are about, and that decision is worth more than the code that follows from it. Write the sentence down first. Then make the machine prove it.
crumbs: 26  Inside Shmup: A Frame of Reference

title: Appendix A — Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua
text:
A.1 The base language
Playdate Lua is Lua 5.4 — same syntax, same semantics, same reference manual — built into a runtime that removes the parts of the standard library that assume a host operating system and adds a handful of conveniences of its own. This appendix is the reference version of the ground Chapter 2 covers by demonstration: if you arrive from desktop Lua, this is the diff; if a listing in this book uses something your Lua does not have, this is where it is accounted for. Everything here is verified against the SDK 3.0.6 reference (Inside Playdate, §5 “Developing in Lua” and §7.1 “Playdate SDK Lua enhancements”) and, where the reference is silent, against the live runtime via the 02-lua-lab example, which prints the answers on screen.
The dialect is Lua 5.4. Everything structural — integer/float distinction, goto, bitwise operators, metatables, coroutines, variable attributes — is present and behaves per the Lua 5.4 manual, with one configuration difference that matters:
Numbers are 32-bit. The SDK reference states it in one line: the runtime is configured to use 32-bit numbers, where desktop Lua defaults to 64. Both number subtypes shrink: integers run from -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 (and wrap around on overflow, as Lua integers always do), and floats are single-precision, good for roughly seven significant decimal digits. In practice this bites in two places. Counters that grow forever are safe as integers (a frame counter at 30 fps takes over two years to exhaust 31 bits) but not as floats: a single-precision float loses the ability to represent every integer beyond 224, so t += 1 on a float silently stops counting at 16,777,216. And accumulated float arithmetic — adding 1/30 to a clock thousands of times — drifts sooner than 64-bit habits expect, which is one more reason the book’s skeleton (Chapter 3) treats frame numbers, not float seconds, as the authoritative clock. Printed floats also show fewer digits than desktop Lua’s, which is cosmetic, but can startle when a debug print shows 0.3333333 where you expected fourteen 3s.
Variable attributes deserve a note because the book uses one of them constantly:
crumbs: A  Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua

title: Appendix A — Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua
text:
<const> works exactly as in standard 5.4 and the SDK’s own documentation recommends it for the local gfx <const> = playdate.graphics idiom — the binding cannot be reassigned and the compiler may treat it as a constant (Chapter 2).
<close> (to-be-closed variables) compiles under pdc and carries standard 5.4 semantics. You will almost never want it: its natural use is deterministic cleanup of OS resources, and the resources it was designed around (io file handles) do not exist here. None of the sixty games behind this book use it.
crumbs: A  Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua

title: Appendix A — Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua
text:
A.2 What is missing
The sandbox removes the standard libraries that reach outside the program, and the loader machinery that assumes a filesystem full of .lua files. Figure 2.3 in Chapter 2 shows the runtime confirming this live.



Table A.1: The removed standard libraries and their Playdate replacements.










Standard Lua
Playdate status
What to use instead




require, package.*
Absent
import (see below)


dofile, loadfile
No role — all code is precompiled into the pdx by pdc; there are no loose .lua files at runtime
import


os.* (time, exit, env, process)
Absent
playdate.getTime(), playdate.getSecondsSinceEpoch(), playdate.getCurrentTimeMilliseconds()


io.* (files, stdin/stdout)
Absent
playdate.file.* for files; playdate.datastore.* for save data (Chapter 17); print still works and goes to the Simulator console


print
Present
— (but see Chapter 18 on why per-frame printing distorts timings)






What survives of the standard library: coroutine, math, string, table, and utf8, plus base functions like pairs, ipairs, pcall, select, tostring, tonumber, assert, and setmetatable. That set is your standard library; if a desktop Lua snippet uses anything else, that is the porting work. When bringing code in from elsewhere, grep it for os%. and io%. first — those two prefixes account for nearly all of it.
crumbs: A  Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua

title: Appendix A — Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua
text:
A.3 import is not require
Files join a Playdate game through import "name", and it differs from require in every way that matters (Chapter 2 demonstrates each):

One shared global environment. There is no per-module scope. A global assigned in any file is visible to every file. Module structure is therefore a convention — this book’s is module-per-concern: one file, one global table (Game, Draw, Input…), everything else local.
Each file runs exactly once. A second import of the same file, from anywhere, does nothing. You never guard against double-loading.
Return values are discarded. The desktop local M = require "mod" idiom has no equivalent; a file that ends in return M silently exports nothing.
It is resolved at compile time. pdc walks the import graph and compiles everything reachable from main.lua into a single main.pdz. You cannot compute a filename and import it at runtime.
Import order is the dependency mechanism. Files execute in import order; a file may read any global defined by files imported before it, including at load time.

The SDK also ships import "CoreLibs/strict", which turns undeclared global reads and writes inside functions into immediate errors — a useful development-time antidote to the one-namespace spelling problem (Chapter 2).
crumbs: A  Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua

title: Appendix A — Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua
text:
A.4 What is added

A.4.1 Compound assignment operators
pdc extends the grammar with the assignment operators standard Lua famously lacks (Inside Playdate §7.1):



Table A.2: Compound assignment operators accepted by pdc.





Operator
Meaning




+= -= *= /=
add, subtract, multiply, divide


//= %=
integer-divide, modulo


<<= >>=
shift left, shift right


&= \|=
bitwise AND, bitwise OR


^=
exponent (not bitwise XOR — Lua’s ^ is power)






They are pure syntactic convenience with one operational caveat: source that uses them no longer parses in desktop Lua tools — linters, formatters, test runners. The shipped games use them freely; code meant to be shared with non-Playdate Lua should not.


A.4.2 Table additions
The SDK extends the table library (§7.1):



Table A.3: SDK additions to the table library.









Function
What it does




table.create(arrayCount, hashCount)
New table with array and hash parts preallocated. The performance tool of the set: growing a table element-by-element in a hot loop is a GC tax you can pay once, up front (Chapter 19).


table.indexOfElement(t, element)
First index of element in an array-style table, or nil. Linear search — fine for menus, wrong for hot loops.


table.getsize(t)
Returns arrayCount, hashCount — the two parts of the table, sized separately.


table.shallowcopy(source, [destination])
Copy one level; nested tables are shared by reference.


table.deepcopy(source)
Recursive copy, including nested tables.








A.4.3 The json global
A json table is available everywhere, no import required: json.decode(string), json.decodeFile(path), json.encode(table), json.encodePretty(table), and json.encodeToFile(...). In this book it appears mostly by implication — playdate.datastore (Chapter 17) speaks JSON on disk, which is why the harness heartbeat of Chapter 18 can be read by a shell script polling with grep.


A.4.4 class() and Object, from CoreLibs
Not a language extension but easy to mistake for one: import "CoreLibs/object" defines a global class() function implementing a single-inheritance object system in plain Lua.
class("Oak", { rings = 0 }).extends(Tree)  -- properties optional

function Oak:init(age)
    Oak.super.init(self, age)   -- chain to parent; note .init(self, ...)
end

local oak = Oak(12)             -- instances: call the class
oak:isa(Tree)                   -- true
oak.className                   -- "Oak"
Every class inherits from Object; ClassName.super names the parent. The one syntactic trap is documented by the SDK itself: chain to the parent initializer as Oak.super.init(self, ...) — the colon form Oak.super:init(...) passes the class as self and misbehaves. Chapter 2 weighs when class() earns its keep against plain tables; the SDK’s sprite system (Chapter 6) is the one place this book requires it, because sprite subclasses extend playdate.graphics.sprite.
crumbs: A  Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua

title: Appendix A — Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua
text:
A.5 Runtime conventions worth internalizing
Three habits of the platform, all from the SDK’s own conventions section, that read as style but function as law:

Arrays are 1-indexed, here as everywhere in Lua, and the entire playdate.* API agrees.
Dot calls tables, colon calls instances. playdate.graphics functions are called with .; object methods with :. Confusing them produces the platform’s signature hard-to-track bug (Chapter 2).
Some APIs return objects, others return value lists — e.g. playdate.geometry.rect.new(...) returns a rect object while rect.fast_intersection(...) returns four numbers. Check the reference before destructuring.

And one habit of this book that is not SDK law but follows from the 32-bit, GC-taxed runtime: cache playdate.graphics (and any other namespace you touch per-frame) in a file-local <const>, keep per-frame allocation near zero, and let Chapter 19 show you what those two rules are worth in milliseconds.
crumbs: A  Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua

title: Appendix B — SDK Coverage Map
text:
B.1 System and runtime
This book claims to teach the Playdate Lua SDK, and a claim like that deserves an audit. This appendix is the audit: every subsection of the API reference in Inside Playdate (§7, SDK 3.0.6) appears in the tables below, mapped to the chapter — and the chapter section, by its heading — where the book covers it, with an honest depth rating. Use it in either direction. Coming from the SDK reference (“what does the book say about playdate.pathfinder?”), scan the leftmost columns for the namespace and follow the cross-reference. Coming from a gap in your own game (“I need a scrolling list”), the area names are descriptive enough to browse.
The depth ratings mean exactly this. Full: the book teaches the area’s working core through the chapter’s buildable example, including at least one gotcha — the standard set by the authoring rules. Introduced: the API is explained and situated — what it is, when to reach for it — with real calls on the page, but the chapter’s example does not lean on it or only part of the area appears. Mentioned: named with enough context to know it exists and where it fits, nothing more. Not covered: absent from the book; every such area gets a sentence at the end of this appendix saying what it is and where to read about it, so the map has no blank edges. Ratings describe the area’s working core, not every last function — the fine print after the tables lists the stray functions inside otherwise-covered areas that the book skips.

title: Appendix B — SDK Coverage Map
text:
B.9 The tally
Sixty-five areas: 39 Full, 11 Introduced, 2 Mentioned, 13 Not covered. Everything a single-player game running on the device touches — graphics, sound, input, timers, persistence, pathfinding, the lifecycle — is taught at Full or Introduced depth. Part VII moved three of those numbers: the stencil family is its own row now, because Chapter 24 builds a light compositor out of it rather than name-checking it; and JSON is promoted from Introduced to Full, because Chapter 25 treats a datastore round-trip’s key semantics as a structural constraint on the save format rather than a footnote. The Not covered column clusters at the edges: connectivity, recorded-media plumbing, and single-function conveniences. Here is what each one is, so you know whether you need it.
Localization (§7.6) is one function: playdate.getSystemLanguage() returns whether the console is set to English or Japanese, so a bilingual game can pick its strings. Accessibility (§7.7) is two reads — getReduceFlashing() and getFlipped() report the user’s system settings; a game with flash-heavy effects should check the former and route it into a master switch like the one Chapter 15 builds. Video (§7.20) plays pre-rendered .pdv files through playdate.graphics.video — for cutscenes, and rarely worth the storage in a 1-bit game. In sound, sample (§7.28) is the raw audio-buffer object beneath sampleplayer (slicing, saving, loading); signal and controlsignal are modulation sources that automate synth parameters from sequencer tracks; the effects family (bitcrusher, ring modulator, one- and two-pole filters, overdrive, delay line) attaches DSP to channels; mic input records from the built-in or headset microphone; and audio output covers headphone detection and the audio device clock. Strings (§7.29, CoreLibs/string) adds UUID() and three whitespace-trimming functions to standard Lua’s string. Math (§7.23) is exactly one function, playdate.math.lerp — this book writes a + (b - a) * t where it needs it. Networking (§7.24, OS 2.7+) brings HTTP and TCP, up to four connections, behind a user-consent prompt; serial communication (§7.33) lets a game receive msg commands over USB; and Playdate Mirror (§7.34) provides callbacks so a game can lighten its load while the Mirror app streams it to a computer. For all of these, the numbered section of Inside Playdate in the left column is the definitive reference — and after twenty-five chapters of this book’s habits, none of them will read as foreign.
Fine print on the partially covered rows: within areas rated Full or Introduced, the book does not use playdate.apiVersion() (§7.2), the calendar half of date & time (getTime, getGMTTime, epoch conversions — tabulated but not exercised in Appendix A), the display transforms setScale, setInverted, setMosaic, setFlipped, and setOffset (§7.16), or the console helpers printTable and where() (§7.14). Each behaves exactly as its reference entry says, and none hides a trap the book’s covered neighbors have not already taught you to expect.

title: Appendix C — Environment Setup
text:
C.1 Installing the SDK
This appendix takes a fresh machine to a working Playdate development environment: SDK installed, pdc on your PATH, Simulator behaving, a device accepting uploads — and, for readers who want to run this book’s figure pipeline or adopt its testing style (Chapter 18, Appendix D), the extra configuration that lets the Simulator run unattended. That last part is where the hard-won details live; everything before it is fifteen minutes of ordinary setup.
Download the SDK for your platform from play.date/dev — it is free, and includes the compiler, the Simulator, CoreLibs, fonts, example projects, and the API reference.
Two pieces of shell configuration make everything downstream nicer, and some of it possible. In your shell startup file (~/.zprofile for zsh, ~/.bash_profile or ~/.bashrc for bash):
PLAYDATE_SDK_PATH tells pdc (and third-party tooling) where the SDK lives; per the SDK docs it is recommended on macOS and effectively required on Linux and Windows for anything Make- or CMake-shaped. Adding bin/ to your PATH lets you run pdc and pdutil from anywhere. Verify with:

title: Appendix C — Environment Setup
text:
macOS — run the SDK installer. It installs to ~/Developer/PlaydateSDK, the path assumed by every script and Makefile in this book.
Windows — extract the archive and run the SDK installer application inside it.
Linux — extract the archive, move the SDK folder somewhere user-writable, and run the setup.sh script inside it.


export PLAYDATE_SDK_PATH="$HOME/Developer/PlaydateSDK"
export PATH="$PLAYDATE_SDK_PATH/bin:$PATH"

pdc --version

title: Appendix C — Environment Setup
text:
C.2 The Simulator, briefly
The Simulator (bin/Playdate Simulator.app on macOS) runs .pdx bundles and emulates the whole console: keyboard keys map to the d-pad and buttons, the on-screen crank can be dragged (or scrolled), and the Playdate and Device menus hold the development tools. The three things to find on day one:

The console window — print() output and Lua error tracebacks land here, not in your terminal.
The Disk folder — the SDK’s Disk/ directory stands in for the device’s storage. Your game’s data directory appears under Disk/Data/<bundleID>/ (more below).
Performance honesty — the Simulator runs your Lua on a desktop CPU enormously faster than the device’s Cortex-M7. It is the right place to develop and the wrong place to conclude your game is fast (Chapter 19).

One behavior shapes all tooling: the Simulator is single-instance. Launching a second copy does not open a second window; it hands the pdx to the running instance and exits. Scripted workflows therefore always kill the Simulator before launching (pkill -9 -f "Playdate Simulator" on macOS) rather than trusting process handles.

title: Appendix C — Environment Setup
text:
C.3 Onto the device
For your own device over USB, development is pleasantly manual:

Connect the Playdate via USB and wake it — press the metal lock button on the top edge.
Run your game in the Simulator.
Choose Upload Game to Device from the Simulator’s Device menu. The game uploads, installs, and launches on the hardware.

If there is no Device menu, the device is locked, asleep, or not actually connected — the menu exists only while the Simulator can see an unlocked Playdate. The same connection also enables Use Device as Controller, which drives the Simulator from the physical crank and buttons — the cheapest way to feel your crank mapping (Chapter 10) before committing to hardware builds. For command-line workflows, pdutil speaks to a connected device directly.
For distribution — your game on someone else’s Playdate — the free path is sideloading: zip the pdx, upload it to a Playdate account at play.date/account/sideload, and the device downloads and installs it over Wi-Fi like any store purchase. Two rules from Chapter 20 bear repeating because they fail silently: the pdxinfo bundleID must be unique (it keys the game’s identity and save directory), and buildNumber must strictly increase — a sideload update with an unchanged buildNumber is not recognized as an update.

title: Appendix C — Environment Setup
text:
C.4 Running the Simulator unattended
Everything above suffices for interactive development. This book’s figure pipeline — and the smoke-testing discipline of Chapter 18 — asks for more: launch the Simulator from a script, let a scripted run play the game with no human attached, read the results off disk, and repeat, dozens of times in a row. Three macOS-specific obstacles stand between you and that, and each produces a distinctive failure.

C.4.1 App Nap and auto-pause
macOS throttles background applications (“App Nap”), and the Simulator additionally pauses itself when its window is not active. Both are correct defaults for a human and fatal for a headless run: the symptom is a game that starts, then freezes for minutes at a time the moment the Simulator is covered by another window — heartbeats stop, timeouts fire, and nothing is wrong with your game. Fix both once, per machine:
defaults write com.panic.PlaydateSimulator NSAppSleepDisabled -bool YES
defaults write com.panic.PlaydateSimulator autoPause -bool NO
The first exempts the Simulator from App Nap; the second stops it pausing when unfocused or occluded. Diagnosis, if you ever suspect a recurrence: a napping Simulator shows ~0.1% CPU in ps -o %cpu while your script’s polls time out.


C.4.2 The SDK 3.0.6 launch form
On SDK 3.0.6, how you launch the Simulator from a script determines whether your game actually runs:
open "$HOME/Developer/PlaydateSDK/bin/Playdate Simulator.app" \
    --args "/absolute/path/to/Game.pdx"
Three properties of that line are load-bearing, and all three were paid for (an entire fleet of smoke-test scripts went quietly dead after an SDK update before the pattern was found):

Explicit path to the app, not open -a "Playdate Simulator". By-name launches load the pdx but never start the game.
No -g (background) flag — same silent non-start.
Absolute path to the pdx in --args. The Simulator’s working directory is not your shell’s.

A window appears in all cases, which is what makes the broken forms so misleading: the failure looks like a hang in your code. It is not; it is the launch form.


C.4.3 The kill/launch/poll cycle
Because the Simulator is single-instance, and because a previous run may have left one alive, scripted runs follow a fixed ceremony — kill, launch fresh, poll for results, kill again:
pkill -9 -f "Playdate Simulator" 2>/dev/null
open "$SIM" --args "$PDX"
# ... poll the game's data directory for output (below) ...
pkill -9 -f "Playdate Simulator" 2>/dev/null
tools/shoot.sh in the book’s repository is the full worked example, documented step by step in Appendix D.

title: Appendix C — Environment Setup
section: C.5 The data directory, and keeping it clean
text:
C.5 The data directory, and keeping it clean
Everything a game writes — playdate.datastore saves, harness heartbeats, error reports — lands in a per-game directory named by the pdxinfo bundleID:
~/Developer/PlaydateSDK/Disk/Data/<bundleID>/
For this book’s examples that means Disk/Data/com.sdwfrost.book.03modes/ and so on, one directory per chapter, which is how the figure pipeline reads each example’s heartbeat (smoke.json) and error report (err.json) from a shell script while the game runs.
Two hygiene rules keep this arrangement honest:

Wipe before scripted runs. A leftover smoke.json from a previous run satisfies “done” checks instantly and lies to you. tools/shoot.sh deletes the example’s data directory before every launch.
Wipe after scripted runs. Test data must not leak into real saves: an autopilot that beats your game in thirty seconds will happily plant a high score no human can match, and it will still be there when you play for pleasure. Delete the directory when the run is over — and during development generally, remember this directory is your save-file state: deleting it is the “fresh install” test for the load-path defaults of Chapter 17.







NoteWindows and Linux



The unattended-Simulator recipe above is macOS-specific because that is where this book’s pipeline ran; the defaults commands and open semantics have no direct equivalents elsewhere. The underlying principles port cleanly, though: launch the Simulator binary directly with the pdx path as an argument, keep one instance alive at a time, and read results from Disk/Data/<bundleID>/ under the SDK directory, which behaves identically on all three platforms.

title: Appendix C — Environment Setup
text:
C.6 The checklist
For a machine ready to work through this book:

Install the SDK; set PLAYDATE_SDK_PATH; put bin/ on PATH.
pdc --version works in a fresh terminal.
Build and run any example: make -C examples/01-hello run.
(For unattended runs) defaults write com.panic.PlaydateSimulator NSAppSleepDisabled -bool YES and ... autoPause -bool NO.
(With hardware) device unlocked + USB → Device ▸ Upload Game to Device works.

title: Appendix D — The Book Harness Reference
text:
D.1 The Shots table
Every figure in this book was captured by a small pipeline that builds an instrumented copy of a chapter’s example, runs it headless in the Simulator under scripted input, photographs named frames, and fails the whole build if the example crashes or stalls. Chapter 1 introduced the two files that ride along in every example; 1 taught the testing philosophy they implement. This appendix is the third pass: the complete reference — every function, file, Makefile target, and protocol step — written so you can lift the pipeline into your own project. None of it is book-specific; it is the shipped games’ smoke-test harness with a camera attached.
The pipeline has four parts, and they meet at two seams:
The seams: a generated file (bookflag.lua) carries build-time configuration into the Lua world, and the game’s data directory (Disk/Data/<bundleID>/) carries results back out. Nothing else crosses the boundary — no sockets, no IPC, no Simulator plugins — which is why the whole thing is portable to any project that can run make and pkill.
Each example’s source/shots.lua defines one global table. Here is a complete, annotated example — the schema is four keys, all optional, though a Shots table with no shots and no script is just an expensive way to do nothing:
Two design points carry most of the value. The script speaks the game’s input language — the table it returns is the same shape as the snapshot Input.gather() builds from real buttons (Chapter 3), so the game cannot tell a bot from a thumb. And because everything is keyed by frame number under a fixed seed and fixed timestep, a shot frame is a coordinate: “frame 200 shows the pause panel” stays true run after run, which is what lets a book commit its screenshots and regenerate them only when the code changes.

title: Appendix D — The Book Harness Reference
text:
Shots = {
    -- RNG seed. The harness calls math.randomseed(seed) at load
    -- time, so every run of this script is bit-identical. Omit it
    -- and figures stop being reproducible on the first math.random.
    seed = 1,

    -- How many frames to run before reporting done. The harness
    -- actually runs to max(last, every frame named in shots), so
    -- this is a floor, not a hard cut.
    last = 390,

    -- The photographs: figure-name -> frame number. The frame is
    -- captured AFTER that frame's update has drawn, and lands in
    -- figures/<example>/<name>.png. Names here and figure
    -- references in the chapter must match exactly.
    shots = {
        ["modes-title"] = 30,
        ["modes-play"]  = 150,
        ["modes-pause"] = 200,
    },

    -- Synthetic input, called once per frame. Whatever table this
    -- returns is handed to the game as this frame's bot input; nil
    -- means "no scripted input this frame". Key names are YOURS --
    -- they mean whatever your Input.gather consumes.
    script = function(frame)
        if frame == 60 then return { confirm = true } end
        if frame > 60 then return Bot.dodge() end   -- read G, decide
    end,
}

title: Appendix D — The Book Harness Reference
text:
D.2 bookharness.lua
The shared runtime is 90 lines, printed here in full:


bookharness.lua

-- The book's test-and-figure harness, adapted from the 47-line smoke harness
-- used across the plaidate games (see Chapter 18). The staging Makefile
-- (example.mk) generates bookflag.lua defining SMOKE_BUILD and SHOT_PREFIX;
-- in release builds SMOKE_BUILD is false and everything here is a no-op.
--
-- Each example's shots.lua defines a global `Shots` table:
--   Shots = {
--       seed  = 1,                        -- math.randomseed for determinism
--       last  = 240,                      -- run this many frames, then report done
--       shots = { title = 20, ... },      -- figure-name -> frame to capture
--       script = function(frame) ... end, -- synthetic input for this frame
--   }

import "bookflag"

Harness = {
    enabled = SMOKE_BUILD,
    counters = {},
    errWritten = false,
}

local lastNeeded = 0

if Harness.enabled and Shots then
    if Shots.seed then math.randomseed(Shots.seed) end
    lastNeeded = Shots.last or 0
    if Shots.shots then
        for _, f in pairs(Shots.shots) do
            if f > lastNeeded then lastNeeded = f end
        end
    end
end

function Harness.count(key, n)
    if not Harness.enabled then return end
    Harness.counters[key] = (Harness.counters[key] or 0) + (n or 1)
end

function Harness.set(key, val)
    if not Harness.enabled then return end
    Harness.counters[key] = val
end

-- Synthetic input for this frame, or nil when a human is playing.
-- Examples consult this at the top of their input gathering, exactly like
-- the autopilot seam in the shipped games.
function Harness.input(frame)
    if not Harness.enabled or not Shots or not Shots.script then return nil end
    return Shots.script(frame)
end

local function heartbeat(frame, done)
    local t = {}
    for k, v in pairs(Harness.counters) do t[k] = v end
    t.frame = frame
    t.done = done or nil
    playdate.datastore.write(t, "smoke")
end

function Harness.frame(frame, updateFn)
    if not Harness.enabled then
        updateFn()
        return
    end
    if frame == 1 then
        playdate.display.setRefreshRate(0) -- unthrottled; runs are frame-indexed
    end
    local ok, err = pcall(updateFn)
    if not ok and not Harness.errWritten then
        Harness.errWritten = true -- keep the FIRST error; later frames repeat symptoms
        playdate.datastore.write({ err = tostring(err) }, "err")
    end
    -- Capture after the frame has drawn, so the shot shows this frame's output.
    if Shots and Shots.shots and playdate.simulator then
        for name, f in pairs(Shots.shots) do
            if f == frame then
                playdate.simulator.writeToFile(playdate.graphics.getDisplayImage(),
                    SHOT_PREFIX .. name .. ".png")
            end
        end
    end
    if frame >= lastNeeded then
        if not Harness.doneWritten then
            Harness.doneWritten = true
            heartbeat(frame, true)
        end
    elseif frame % 90 == 0 then
        heartbeat(frame, false)
    end
end

The public API is four functions and a flag:







Member
Role




Harness.enabled
SMOKE_BUILD from the generated bookflag.lua. False in release builds, making every other member a no-op.


Harness.input(frame)
The bot seam. Returns Shots.script(frame) in instrumented builds, nil otherwise — call it first in your input gathering and fall back to real buttons on nil.


Harness.frame(frame, updateFn)
The wrapper. Runs one frame of the real game inside pcall, captures any shots due this frame, and emits heartbeats. Called from playdate.update.


Harness.count(key, n)
Increment a named counter (default 1). Counters ride along in every heartbeat — “count everything” is the telemetry habit of 1.


Harness.set(key, val)
Set a counter to an absolute value — for gauges (mode name, boss HP) rather than tallies.



Reading the listing top to bottom, the load-time block seeds the RNG and computes lastNeeded — the run length — as the larger of Shots.last and the latest named shot, so a typo’d shot frame extends the run instead of silently never firing. Harness.frame then does the per-frame work, and its details are the accumulated lessons of the shipped games’ harnesses:

Unthrottled refresh. On frame 1 it calls playdate.display.setRefreshRate(0) — run as fast as the host CPU allows. Because all logic is frame-indexed with a fixed DT (Chapter 3), the run is identical to a 30 fps run, just faster in wall-clock: a 390-frame script finishes in a couple of seconds.
First error wins. The update runs inside pcall; on failure the error is written to the err datastore once (errWritten). Later frames usually repeat the same symptom — the first traceback is the one that names the cause.
Capture after draw. Shots are taken after updateFn returns, so the PNG shows exactly what that frame put on screen. playdate.simulator.writeToFile writes a real host-side PNG (unlike datastore.writeImage, which writes the device .pdi format regardless of the extension you ask for).
Heartbeats, then done. Every 90 frames the counters are written to the smoke datastore with the current frame number; at lastNeeded the same table goes out with done = true. A watching script can distinguish “healthy but running,” “crashed” (err.json exists), and “hung” (heartbeat frame stopped advancing).

The wrapper in each example’s main.lua is the standard two-liner — and note the import order above it: import "shots" must precede import "bookharness", because the harness reads Shots at load time to seed the RNG before any game code runs:
local realUpdate = playdate.update
function playdate.update()
    frame = frame + 1
    Harness.frame(frame, realUpdate)
end

title: Appendix D — The Book Harness Reference
section: D.3 example.mk: one source, two builds
text:
D.3 example.mk: one source, two builds
Each example’s Makefile is two lines — NAME := Modes and include ../../example.mk — and the shared rules produce two flavors of pdx from the same source:







Target
What it builds




make book
Instrumented build in build/<slug>/book/: source staged, bookharness.lua copied in, and a generated bookflag.lua containing SMOKE_BUILD = true and SHOT_PREFIX = "<abs path to figures/<slug>/raw>/". This is what shoot.sh runs.


make release
Clean build in out/<NAME>.pdx with a generated SMOKE_BUILD = false flag — the harness compiles in but does nothing. What a player would get.


make run
release, then opens the pdx in the Simulator (using the 3.0.6-safe launch form of Appendix C).



The generated-file trick is the point to steal. bookflag.lua does not exist in anyone’s source/ directory; the Makefile writes it into the staged copy at build time. That gives the Lua side compile-time configuration — a boolean and an absolute host path — without a config file to forget to edit, and it guarantees a release build cannot accidentally ship with the harness armed, because the release rule generates the false flag unconditionally. The same mechanism carries the shot directory: SHOT_PREFIX bakes the host filesystem path for playdate.simulator.writeToFile into the build, which is how shots land directly in the book’s figures/ tree.
Staging itself is one cp -r source/* … — and the -r is a scar, not a habit:
# Staging always uses `cp -r`: a bare `cp source/*` silently skips
# subdirectories and pdc will happily build the incomplete bundle.
A bare cp copies the top-level files, warns on stderr nobody reads, and skips images/ and sounds/ — and pdc then compiles the incomplete bundle without complaint, producing a pdx whose failures (missing images, silent nil sounds) point everywhere except at the copy. Every staged pipeline in the shipped games hit some version of this before standardizing on cp -r.

title: Appendix D — The Book Harness Reference
text:
D.4 tools/shoot.sh: the protocol
tools/shoot.sh <example-slug> runs one example end to end. It is ~60 lines of bash; the steps, in order, with the reasoning:

Derive the coordinates. From the slug: the bundle ID (com.sdwfrost.book. + slug with dashes removed), and from it the data directory Disk/Data/<bundleID>/; the pdx path from NAME in the example’s Makefile; the figure directory figures/<slug>/. Everything else is mechanical once these are right — and a typo’d bundle ID means polling an empty directory forever, the trap flagged back in Chapter 1.
Build. make book. A compile error stops the run here, with pdc’s message.
Kill the Simulator. pkill -9 -f "Playdate Simulator" — it is single-instance, and a survivor from the last run would swallow the launch.
Wipe state. Delete the example’s data directory and the raw figure directory. Stale smoke.json or leftover PNGs from a previous run would satisfy every check below and lie.
Launch. open "$SIM" --args "$PDX" — explicit app path, no -g, absolute pdx path: the 3.0.6-safe form (Appendix C).
Poll. Every 2 seconds, up to 90: if err.json exists, print it and fail — the game crashed, and the first error is already in the file. If smoke.json contains "done":true, succeed. If the loop expires, print the last heartbeat (which says how far the run got) and fail as a hang.
Kill again. Success or failure, no Simulator outlives the run.
Wipe the datastore. Test data must not leak into real saves (Appendix C).
Verify the shots. Every run must produce at least one PNG; shoot.sh fails loudly if the raw directory is empty — the defense against a Shots.shots table that quietly stopped matching any frame.
Upscale and stamp. Each raw 400x240 capture is doubled to 800x480 with magick -filter point -resize 200% — nearest neighbor, so every screen pixel becomes a crisp 2x2 block, no smoothing — then the raw directory is removed and a .stamp file records success.

The stamp is what the book’s top-level make figures keys on: an example is re-shot only if its sources (or the shared harness, or shoot.sh itself) are newer than its stamp, and the whole figures target runs .NOTPARALLEL because the Simulator is single-instance. Shooting is therefore also the book’s continuous smoke test — a chapter whose example crashes cannot render with stale figures; the build stops at step 6 instead.

title: Appendix D — The Book Harness Reference
section: D.5 Adapting it to your project
text:
D.5 Adapting it to your project
The pipeline assumes almost nothing. To reuse it:

Route your input through one snapshot function with the bot consulted first (Chapter 3, Chapter 9). This is the only change to game code, and it is the same seam an autopilot needs

— the camera is a free rider on your test rig.

Copy bookharness.lua (rename freely). If you do not need figures, delete the shot block and SHOT_PREFIX and you have the shipped games’ pure smoke harness.
Reproduce the two-build staging in your build system: stage source with cp -r, generate the flag file into the staged copy, pdc from there. Any build tool can do this; the load-bearing part is that the flag is generated, never hand-edited.
Copy shoot.sh and change three variables at the top — bundle ID prefix, SDK path, output directory. Keep the kill/wipe/poll ceremony intact; every line of it is a failure mode someone paid for.
Write a Shots script per thing you want tested or photographed. Start with last = 300 and no shots: that alone — run for ten seconds under a seeded bot without crashing — catches a remarkable fraction of regressions.

What you get for the effort is the meta-payoff this book has been demonstrating on itself: documentation whose screenshots cannot lie, and a test that plays your game every time you type make.

title: Appendix E — The Case-Study Games
text:
E.1 Standalones
The code quoted in this book’s sidebars comes from real, shipped Playdate games — more than sixty of them, built by the author on the house style this book teaches. This appendix is the cast list: what each game is, and where and why the book quotes it. Source for the published games lives in the github.com/plaidate organization; quotes in the chapters carry repo/path.lua:line attributions so you can read every excerpt in context.
The catalog is in two halves: sixteen standalone games, then the seven collections — shared engines with a fleet of games on top, forty-nine games between them — plus one reusable module. Five of those collections get a whole chapter to themselves in Part VII. Screenshots are the games’ own repository screenshot.png files, upscaled 2x nearest-neighbor like every other figure in the book.

title: Appendix E — The Case-Study Games
text:
E.1.1 Molt
An arkanoidvania: you are a crab, your carapace is the paddle, and a bounced pearl smashes coral across a connected undersea world of 28 rooms, 6 zones, and 7 bosses. The largest game in the fleet, and the book’s favorite witness for “does this pattern survive contact with a real game.”






Figure E.1: Molt: paddle, pearl, and a room of the connected world.



Cited in: Chapter 2 (the twenty-import main.lua that demonstrates module-per-concern at scale), Chapter 3 (the if/elseif flavor of the mode machine, and the EMA update-cost wrapper that feeds Chapter 19), Chapter 12 (fourteen all-synth voices in one sfx.lua, zero audio files), Chapter 13 (its step-clock music module), Chapter 17 (save.lua quoted as a complete file — the nil-coalescing load pattern), and Chapter 18, where its 47-line smoke harness is the direct ancestor of this book’s own (Appendix D). Its full-game-beating autopilot — BFS recovery, offset aiming, persistent boss wounds — is the existence proof behind that chapter’s claims.


E.1.2 Blood and Whine
A mosquito continuing her bloodline: nectar to survive, blood to breed, and every host can end you. A full procedural 1-bit art sweep gave it the book’s favorite dither lesson.






Figure E.2: Blood and Whine: procedural 1-bit art, all dither.



Cited in: Chapter 4 — its draw.lua gray(d) helper, and the scar behind that chapter’s central warning: setDitherPattern’s argument is transparency, not darkness (low = dark), a polarity that cost this game an art pass.


E.1.3 Fightin’ Chitin
A one-on-one 2D fighting game starring the insect world’s real brawlers: two attack buttons, motion inputs, a crank-driven Frenzy super, and a six-fighter archetype roster.






Figure E.3: Fightin’ Chitin: two bugs enter.



Cited in: Chapter 4 (HUD-versus-stage dither discipline in stage.lua), Chapter 10 (the character-select wheel in select.lua), Chapter 14 (fighter AABBs in fight.lua — the strict-inequality overlap test), and Chapter 15 (KO hitstop: bodies hang a beat before falling).


E.1.4 Clam Jumper
An ocean-bed take on the 1982 arcade game Claim Jumper: pick sea star, octopus, or ray — each with different verbs — against a rival species in a connected coral maze.






Figure E.4: Clam Jumper: species versus species in the coral maze.



Cited in: Chapter 9 (its input module’s snapshot shape) and Chapter 16, where its Util.pathNext BFS — one flood fill shared by the enemy AI and the autopilot — is the chapter’s founding example of “the bot is just another consumer of the game’s own pathfinding.”


E.1.5 Weasel War Dance
A rhythm game about the mustelid family’s greatest trick: the frenzied “war dance” stoats and weasels really do perform. Eight mustelids, small to large, dance to a clock-driven step sequencer.






Figure E.5: Weasel War Dance: chart, dancer, and the beat clock.



Cited in: Chapter 13, three times — its conductor.lua is the book’s reference implementation of the zero-drift step sequencer: a clock that schedules notes by beat arithmetic rather than trusting callbacks to arrive on time. A rhythm game is the genre least able to tolerate drift, which is what makes its conductor the load-bearing witness.


E.1.6 Archer’s Zoomie Circuit
Wind up the whippet. A one-button-and-crank racing game starring the author’s actual dog (who also, by seniority, supplied this book’s cover). Cited in: Chapter 15 — notably as the only game in the fleet built on playdate.timer, which is exactly why that chapter trusts it for the timer coverage the other sixty-three games skip.


E.1.7 Bauble
A 1-bit color-matching bubble shooter: crank to aim, fire, pop clusters of three or more. Cited in: Chapter 3 — its gamestate.lua documents the whole legal-mode enum in a comment at the declaration, the convention the skeleton adopts.


E.1.8 Bearing
A tilt-controlled marble maze: the accelerometer rolls a steel bearing through a labyrinth, past holes, against the clock. Cited in: Chapter 11 — accelerometer smoothing from its main.lua, the difference between raw tilt (jittery) and playable tilt.


E.1.9 Flock
A sheepdog trial: steer the dog with the crank, creep and bark to drive a mixed flock of sheep, goats, ducks and geese past rocks, walls and ponds through a funnel race into the pen before the clock runs out — four dog personas, eight boids-driven breeds, twelve trials.






Figure E.6: Flock: the dog works a scattering flock down the funnel toward the pen, the camera zoomed out to hold them all.



Cited in: Chapter 7, as the book’s one zoom-to-fit camera — the fighting-game / Samurai Shodown pattern that fits a bounding box of the whole cast (dog plus every unpenned animal) instead of chasing a single target. It is the chapter’s witness for why a zooming camera on the Playdate cannot ride the setDrawOffset sandwich (no hardware scale), must bake the zoom into a hand-rolled world→screen transform, and therefore draws its entire cast as parametric 1-bit geometry that stays crisp at any scale.


E.1.10 The supporting bestiary
Seven more standalones inform chapters without being quoted line by line; their lessons arrived as design rules rather than excerpts:

Squirl — a squirrel Metroidvania/parkour game; the Magic Acorn swaps you between six squirrel species, each a traversal key. Its full-game-beating autopilot (31 rooms, 6 bosses) is part of the evidence base for Chapter 18’s claim that autopilots scale to whole games.
AnEnemy — sea-anemone territorial warfare: crank-engorged acrorhagi, a feed-versus-fight economy, tides. Its four-strain campaign ships with a full-campaign autopilot in the same tradition.
Bin Night — a tag-team trash heist (raccoon, ibis, cat versus the dawn garbage truck). Contributed the “summon what the test needs” autopilot style and heartbeat time-series tracing to the toolbox Chapter 18 teaches.
Peckish — a killdeer foraging the tideline: a wet-band worm economy behind receding waves, with the crank performing the broken-wing lure. A study in mapping one crank to one strong verb (Chapter 10’s design rule).
Nectar Rush — territorial-hummingbird feeder defense over a shared nectar pool; crank to sip. Its autopilot fails in stages — deliberately losing in controlled ways — a testing idea that resurfaces in Chapter 18.
Fairway and Roost — top-down golf across larger-than-screen procedural holes, and an egg-gathering aerial lance-jousting arcade — round out the fleet, exercising the big-world camera patterns of Chapter 7 and the flap-physics tuning habits of Chapter 14 respectively.

title: Appendix E — The Case-Study Games
text:
E.2 The collections
Seven repositories are not single games but engines with fleets on top. When a chapter quotes vec/ or tiles/core/, it is quoting code that a dozen games run through every frame — the strongest battle-testing in the catalog. Six of the seven — Phosphor, Tiles, Voxel, Dither, Lore, and Shmup — are read end to end in Part VII, each in a chapter of its own with the engine vendored into a runnable example.

E.2.1 Phosphor
Sixteen vector arcade games — white beam lines on black, the way the cabinets drew them — on one thin shared library, vec/.






Figure E.7: Gyre, from the Phosphor collection: pure crank, pure vectors.



Cited in: Chapter 21 — an entire chapter walks the vec/ engine module by module (the mathematics, the code, and all sixteen games) and is this collection’s primary citation. Also Chapter 5 (the beams.lua stroke font — text drawn as line segments, no bitmaps), Chapter 8 (its mat.lua rotation matrices, proj.lua near-plane clipping, and shapes.lua asteroid generation are that chapter’s spine), Chapter 10 (Gyre, the collection’s signature crank game — 1:1 angular control), Chapter 15 (the shared particle pool in vec/fx.lua), and Chapter 17 (the cabinet-style shared attract mode and its high-score table in attract.lua).


E.2.2 Classics
Both volumes of Code the Classics, ported as original Lua implementations sharing one core — a Pong, a Frogger, a Centipede, a soccer game, a brawler, and five more. Less quoted than lived-in: this port marathon (ten games in one push) is where the headless smoke-testing workflow was first hardened — the pcall-to-datastore error trap, the writeToFile visual debugging, and the asset-conversion checks that resurface across Chapter 5, Chapter 18, and Appendix C.


E.2.3 Voxel
Original 1-bit voxel games on one thin engine — a Voxatron-style world of shaded columns in a 3/4 projection.






Figure E.8: Lob, from the Voxel collection: wind the crank, release the shot.



Cited in: Chapter 23 — an entire chapter walks the core/ engine module by module (the render-once background with exact hidden-face culling, Vox.carve’s dirty-strip craters, the occlusion ghost, both physics families, the shared aim solver, the Kit.run cabinet, and all ten games) and is this collection’s primary citation. Also Chapter 8 (the two-add 3/4 projection, and the collection’s design rule quoted there: a voxel game must use height and volume — dig, lob, climb, flood, fall — to earn the engine), Chapter 10 (Lob’s wind-and-release crank mapping), and Chapter 16 (Lob again: area-of-effect tools must check for friendlies, thrower included).


E.2.4 Tiles
Original tile-and-sprite games built the way you would build for the NES or Game Boy: a fixed 16px grid, a scrolling camera, and four games (Blast, Spirit, Relic, Burrow) on the shared core.






Figure E.9: Blast, from the Tiles collection: a Bomberman on the 16px grid.



Cited in: Chapter 22 — an entire chapter walks the core/ engine module by module (the tile-cache cost model, the physics, the BFS field, the camera, and all four games) and is this collection’s primary citation. Also Chapter 5 (the fully procedural tileset in tmap.lua: four games shipped without a drawing tool ever being opened), Chapter 7 (tcam.lua’s follow-and-clamp camera and the pre-rendered background repaint strategy), Chapter 12 (tsnd.lua noise sweeps), Chapter 14 (tphys.lua — the fixed-DT tile physics and 1px substep at that chapter’s core, plus Blast’s walkable-bomb isSolid and Burrow’s falling-boulder variant), and Chapter 16 (Blast’s radius checks). Relic’s save-versioning appears in Chapter 17.


E.2.5 Dither
The shade engine: dither ramps, dynamic lights, shadows, transitions, and Super Scaler pseudo-3D on one core, with three games that each use shade — Sprint (a Super Sprint-style racer with day/dusk/night headlight racing), Glim (a firefly-keeper whose lantern wick is the resource), and Skimmer (a Space Harrier-lineage dragonfly over a pond).






Figure E.10: Glim, from the Dither collection: the lantern is a light, the light is the game.



Cited in: Chapter 24 — an entire chapter walks the core/ engine module by module (the 17-level Bayer/noise ramps, the stencil-gated three-band light compositor and its Light.at pixels-equal-logic query, shadows and Bayer transitions, the mip ladders, depth queue, and perspective floor of the scaler, and all three games) and is this collection’s primary citation. It builds directly on Chapter 4’s dither-ladder foundations, and its plane-cull war story is that chapter’s ordering-dependency lesson.


E.2.6 Lore
The RPG engine: story state over a chunked tile world — a big walkable overworld, people to talk to, a party that grows, and battles in two grammars (menu-driven turns in a scene, real-time swings in the field) over one shared character sheet. Three games: Slice (the vertical slice and living engine demo — a chunked overworld, NPCs, a shop, a chest, a battle, a save), Shrew (Dragon Quest grammar: a shrew knight must eat half her body weight by dawn — overworld, town, boat, two-floor burrow, the Mole Tyrant), and Stoat (Secret of Mana grammar: charge swings, pounces, telegraphed rats, and a Rat King who stops mid-fight to talk).






Figure E.11: Shrew, from the Lore collection: the dawn meter, the chunked overworld, and twelve grams to eat.



Cited in: Chapter 25 — an entire chapter walks the core/ engine module by module (the chunked LRU map cache, the state stack that makes menus-over-dialogs-over-shops free, the coroutine screenplay runner whose primitives also write the smoke autopilot, the one character sheet under both battle engines, the pattern-and-order-list music with its resume-mid-phrase stinger, and all three games) and is this collection’s primary citation. It builds on Chapter 22 (lmap descends from tmap: same char grids, same write barrier, chunked), Chapter 7 (lcam is tcam plus a scripted pan), Chapter 17 (its lstate ledger is the string-key JSON lesson made structural), and Chapter 18, whose autopilot argument it takes to the limit — including the war story of the bot that farmed a boss’s summons for 57 kills without ever touching the boss, and tools/headless.lua, a stubbed-SDK Lua runner that beats a whole RPG in two seconds.


E.2.7 Shmup
A 1-bit shoot-’em-up engine built around the frame of reference — what is nailed down and what moves — with one game per frame: Nova Strike (vertical: the world falls past a screen-locked ship, in the line of Xevious), Ravine (side: a forced-scroll cave-flyer with Scramble-style terrain, draining fuel and ballistic bombs), and Skimmer (free: the level is a fixed-width place with two ends and the camera chases you, in the line of Uridium — a different Skimmer from Dither’s dragonfly; the fleet reuses a good name). Games on it are almost entirely data: an enemy type composes one movement behavior with one fire behavior, a level is a spawn timeline, and the whole paradigm is a single string in a table.
Cited in: Chapter 26, which reads the engine end to end and vendors it into that chapter’s example. Its house rule is worth stating on its own: solid white sprites on black space, code-drawn, because that is the lesson a 1-bit OpenTyrian port teaches — at bullet size the eye cannot resolve a dither pattern, so it resolves the average, and the average of a 50% dither is exactly the grey smudge you most need your bullets not to be (Chapter 4).

title: Appendix E — The Case-Study Games
text:
E.3 Modules

E.3.1 midiplayer
Not a game: a reusable MIDI playback module (GM-lite patch bank, track mapper, SMF generator) wrapped in a mixer-style demo app. Cited in: Chapter 13 for the SDK-sequence side of the sequences-versus-step-clocks argument — and, less gloriously, in Chapter 1: its tools/smoke.sh carries the warning comment about the SDK 3.0.6 Simulator launch form, the scar that protects every build script in this book (Appendix C).

title: Appendix E — The Case-Study Games
text:
E.4 Reading the sources
Every plaidate repository follows the conventions this book teaches — source/ with module-per-concern files, a Makefile, a smoke-test harness, a screenshot.png — so the skills transfer directly: pick any game cited in a chapter you have just read, clone it, make run, and find the quoted line. The fleet is the book’s bibliography, and unlike most bibliographies, it compiles.
